tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.details:
Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.
 - std::vector<int, std::allocator<int> >::reserve(unsigned long): 3 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_1>>
 - std::vector<int, std::allocator<int> >::resize(unsigned long): 2 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_2>>
 - std::vector<int, std::allocator<int> >::vector(unsigned long, std::allocator<int> const&): 2 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_3>>
 - unknown: 3 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_4>>
hint.title: CALL instructions

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
 - VZEROUPPER: 3 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_1>>
hint.title: Complex instructions

hint.workaround: Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.
hint.title: Conversion instructions
Type of elements and instruction set
Matching between your function (in the source code) and the binary function
hint.txt:
The binary function does not contain any FP arithmetical operations.
The binary function is loading 208 bytes.
The binary function is storing 232 bytes.

expert.title: General properties
expert.txt:
nb instructions    : 231
nb uops            : 246
loop length        : 990
used x86 registers : 16
used mmx registers : 0
used xmm registers : 2
used ymm registers : 0
used zmm registers : 0
nb stack references: 21

expert.help: Front-end corresponds to instructions fetching and decoding into macro/micro operations
expert.title: Front-end
expert.txt:
MACRO FUSION NOT POSSIBLE
FIT IN UOP CACHE
micro-operation queue: 61.75 cycles
front end            : 61.75 cycles

expert.help: Back-end corresponds to macro/micro operations execution (includes allocating registers, fetching operands etc.)
expert.title: Back-end
expert.txt:
| P0    | P1    | P2    | P3    | P4    | P5    | P6    | P7
----------------------------------------------------------------------
uops   | 28.75 | 28.67 | 26.50 | 26.17 | 43.00 | 28.83 | 28.75 | 26.33
cycles | 28.75 | 28.67 | 26.50 | 26.17 | 43.00 | 28.83 | 28.75 | 26.33

Execution ports to units layout:
 - P0 (256 bits): VPU, BRU, ALU, DIV/SQRT
 - P1 (256 bits): ALU, VPU
 - P2 (256 bits): store address, load
 - P3 (256 bits): store address, load
 - P4 (256 bits): store data
 - P5: ALU
 - P6: ALU, BRU
 - P7: store address

Cycles executing div or sqrt instructions: NA

expert.help: Proportion of vectorizable instructions that are actually vectorized. Higher is better.
expert.title: Vectorization ratios
expert.txt:
all     : 16%
load    : 0%
store   : 11%
mul     : NA (no mul vectorizable/vectorized instructions)
add-sub : 50%
fma     : NA (no fma vectorizable/vectorized instructions)
div/sqrt: NA (no div/sqrt vectorizable/vectorized instructions)
other   : 19%

expert.help: Effective/average relative length/width used in vector/SIMD registers. Higher is better.
expert.title: Vector efficiency ratios
expert.txt:
all     : 26%
load    : 22%
store   : 25%
mul     : NA (no mul vectorizable/vectorized instructions)
add-sub : 37%
fma     : NA (no fma vectorizable/vectorized instructions)
div/sqrt: NA (no div/sqrt vectorizable/vectorized instructions)
other   : 26%

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 61.75 cycles. At this rate:
 - 5% of peak load performance is reached (3.37 out of 64.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
 - 11% of peak store performance is reached (3.76 out of 32.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks
expert.txt:
Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 61.75 to 43.00 cycles (1.44x speedup).

header:
Warnings:
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [ENDBR64] is unknown
 - Detected a function call instruction: ignoring called function instructions.
Rerun with --follow-calls=append to include them to analysis  or with --follow-calls=inline to simulate inlining.
0% of peak computational performance is used (0.00 out of 32.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
- Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 61.75 to 16.25 cycles (3.80x speedup).

gain.workaround:
- Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
 - Make array accesses unit-stride:
  * If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA)
gain.details:
16% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
 - 0% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
 - 11% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
 - 50% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
 - 19% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized function can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your function is not vectorized.
Only 26% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 61.75 to 9.66 cycles (6.39x speedup).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.
